policy_statement: Reduce water consumption per person by 30% by 2030.
mechanism_description: Implement water efficiency standards and related measures to reduce per-person water consumption.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Water-efficiency measures inherently deliver the same water services (sanitation, irrigation, hygiene) with less freshwater withdrawal and distribution per person, i.e., reduced resource input per unit service.
primary_causal_pathway: Water-using end demand → ↓ water production/distribution + ↓ wastewater treatment + ↓ hot-water heating energy → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Households/buildings/community reduce water use through efficiency standards (e.g., fixtures/appliances), ordinances, AMI-enabled feedback, incentives, and conservation programs.  
(2) Mediating process: Lower volumetric demand reduces (a) electricity/fuel used to pump, treat, and distribute potable water, (b) energy used for wastewater conveyance and treatment, and (c) energy used to heat water at end use.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced upstream and onsite energy use leads to lower CO₂e emissions from grid electricity and fuels (with magnitude depending on local water system energy intensity and hot-water share).
dominant_pathway_test: Reducing per-capita water use most strongly lowers energy consumption for water supply/wastewater treatment and especially water heating, thereby reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
(1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main climate effect is reduced energy demand across the urban water cycle (and hot-water heating), which reduces GHG emissions.  
(2) Why this primary over alternatives: While water savings also improve drought resilience and reduce withdrawals, those benefits are not the single largest climate-relevant effect across “any policy using this mechanism”; emissions reductions from avoided energy use are the most direct and generally primary climate linkage.  
(3) Inherent secondary causal chain(s):  
- Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism is explicitly about delivering the same water services with less water input (per-capita water reduction).  
(4) Edge cases applied: If a system is gravity-fed with very low energy intensity and hot-water impacts are minimal, mitigation benefits could be smaller; however, the mechanism still generally targets energy-associated water services and remains mitigation-dominant in typical urban contexts.  
(5) Label applicability check: All instances of “water efficiency standards → per-capita water reduction” necessarily reduce water inputs (resource efficiency) and commonly reduce energy use/emissions (mitigation), so the primary/secondary labels apply across the class.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In locations where water supply/wastewater energy intensity is extremely low and policies are framed/implemented primarily for drought preparedness, an Adaptation framing could be locally salient; however, the mechanism as defined most consistently maps to emissions reduction via reduced energy for water services, with Resource Efficiency inherently co-produced.
co_benefits: Improved drought resilience/water security; reduced strain on water and wastewater infrastructure; potential household cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: If accompanied by binding ordinances, pricing (tiered rates), or mandatory efficiency standards, the instrument_type could shift from target-setting to regulatory-mandate/pricing-mechanism/performance-standard; the excerpt alone is a goal statement.
